subject: Physics
solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{F}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_0}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ 
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}_0}{\mathrm{~m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}}$ 
$\quad\int \mathrm{dv}=\int_0^{\mathrm{t}} \frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{bt}} \mathrm{dt}$
$\Rightarrow v=\frac{F}{m}\left[\frac{-1}{b}\right]\left[e^{-b t}\right]_0^t$
$\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{F}{m b}\left[e^{-b t}\right]$
$v=0 \text { at } t=0$
and $\quad v \rightarrow \frac{F}{m b}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$
So, velocity increases continuously and attains a maximum value of $v=\frac{F}{m b}$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$.

subject: Physics
options: Rope will break while climbing upward
Rope will break while going downward
solution:
Let a1 be the acceleration of monkey while moving down.

F.B.D of monkey while moving downward

Using Newton's second law of motion,

mg-T=ma1

∴  500-T=50×4⇒T=300 N

Let a2 be the acceleration of monkey while moving up.

F.B.D of monkey while moving up

Using Newton's second law of motion

T-mg=ma2

⇒  T-500=50×5

⇒T=750 N

Breaking strength of rope =750-300=350 N

∴  Rope will break while monkey is moving upward.

solution:
It is given that 

 v=10x m s-1

Force is defined by F=mdvdt   ...(i)

Multiplying and dividing equation (i) by dx

F=mdvdtdxdx⇒F=mdxdtdvdx=mvdvdx   ...(ii)

Finding the values of the terms in equation (ii)
dvdx=102x=5x
vdvdx=a=10x×5x=50 m s-2

Hence, the force is 
F=12×50=25 N.

solution: Case-1 : No friction $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g} \sin \theta \\ & \ell=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~g} \sin \theta) \mathrm{t}_1^2 \\ & \mathrm{t}_1=\sqrt{\frac{2 \ell}{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta}} \end{aligned}$ Case-2 : With friction $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{g} \sin \theta-\mu \mathrm{g} \cos \theta \\ & \ell=\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{~g} \sin \theta-\mu \mathrm{g} \cos \theta) \mathrm{t}_2^2 \\ & \sqrt{\frac{2 \ell}{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta-\mu g \cos \theta}}=\mathrm{n} \sqrt{\frac{2 \ell}{\mathrm{g} \sin \theta}} \\ & \mu=1-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2} \end{aligned}$

mgRsinα=12mv2⇒mv2R=2mgsinα

In the radial direction,

N-mgsinα=mv2R⇒N=3mgsinα

Now the ratio of centripetal force and normal reaction is,

A=mv2RN=2mgsinα3mgsinα=23.  Therefore, A is constant.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let the length of the inclined plane be $s$.

For the smooth inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_1 = g \sin \theta$.

The time taken is $t_1 = \dfrac{t}{2}$. Using the second equation of motion $s = ut + \dfrac{1}{2}at^2$ with $u = 0$:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} (g \sin \theta) \left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} g \sin \theta t^2$

For the rough inclined plane, the acceleration of the block is $a_2 = g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta)$.

The time taken is $t_2 = t$. Using the equation of motion again:

$s = \dfrac{1}{2} g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta) t^2$

Equating the two expressions for $s$:

$\dfrac{1}{8} g \sin \theta t^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} g(\sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta) t^2$

$\dfrac{1}{4} \sin \theta = \sin \theta - \mu \cos \theta$

Given $\theta = 45^{\circ}$, we have $\sin 45^{\circ} = \cos 45^{\circ} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. Substituting these values:

$\dfrac{1}{4} = 1 - \mu$

$\mu = 1 - \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75$

We are given $\mu = \dfrac{\alpha}{100}$.

$\dfrac{\alpha}{100} = 0.75 \Rightarrow \alpha = 75$

Answer: $75$

subject: Physics
solution:
The particle experiences downward gravitational force (mg) and upward resistive force (-kv).
The equation of motion is $m\frac{dv}{dt} = mg - kv$.
Initially at $t=0$, $v=0$ gives maximum acceleration $a_0 = g$.
As velocity increases, the resistive force increases, reducing the net acceleration.
Eventually the particle reaches terminal velocity $v_t = \frac{mg}{k}$ when acceleration becomes zero.
The velocity-time graph shows an increasing curve starting from origin with decreasing slope, asymptotically approaching the terminal velocity.
This behavior matches option (1), which displays a curve starting at (0,0) with steep initial slope that gradually flattens.

subject: Physics
Physics
solution:
From question,
Mass of body, $m=5 \mathrm{~kg}$
Velocity at $t=0$,
$$
u=(6 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}
$$
Velocity at $t=10 \mathrm{~s}$,
$$
v=+6 \hat{j} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$
Force, $F=$ ?
Acceleration, $a=\frac{v-u}{t}$
$$
=\frac{6 \hat{j}-(6 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j})}{10}=\frac{-3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}}{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
$$
Force, $F=m a$
$$
=5 \times \frac{(-3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j})}{5}=(-3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) N
$$

subject: Physics
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
When elevator is moving with uniform speed T=Fg, as the net force along the vertical direction should be zero.

Therefore, Statement-I is true.

Now, when elevator is going down with increasing speed, its acceleration is downward.
Hence, 

W-N=ma⇒W-N=Wg×a

⇒N=W1-ag

Clearly, normal reaction is less than weight. So Statement-II is false.

v→=dr→dt=ddt10ti^+15t2j^+7k^= 10i^+30tj^

And, the acceleration of the particle is given by

a→=dv→dt=ddt10i^+30tj^=30j^

Hence, the force on the particle can be written as

F→=ma→=30mj^
